coredata.dc:title: Mechanically robust and fast room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane constructed by coordination bond and hydrogen bond with antibacterial and photoluminescence functions
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yupeng
Jin, Yong
Zeng, Wenhua
Zhou, Rong
Shang, Xiang
Shi, Liangjie
Bai, Long
Lai, Chenxu
coredata.dc:description: It is still a great challenge to synthesize waterborne polyurethane (WPU) with excellent mechanical performance and fast room-temperature self-healing ability because of the mutually exclusive nature of these properties. Herein, a room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane based on Zn2+-carboxyl coordination bond and hydrogen bond was prepared by adding zinc ion buffer solution to WPU emulsion. Due to the synergistic effect of dynamic coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds, the optimized sample (WPU-Zn-3) exhibited excellent mechanical properties (a tensile strength of 7.26 MPa, a elongation at break of 855 % and an exceptional toughness of 26.76 MJ/m3) and fast room-temperature self-healing capacity under ethanol induction (a high healing efficiency of 88 % within 4 h), showing a good balance between fast room temperature healing ability and excellent mechanical properties. Interestingly, this method also works for nickel ions but not for iron and aluminum ions. Furthermore, compared with zinc chloride and zinc nitrate, the waterborne polyurethane containing zinc acetate showed the best comprehensive performance. Due to the presence of zinc ions, the sterilization rates of WPU-Zn-3 film samples against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus were 97.1 % and 97.8 %, respectively. Specially, we introduced graphene quantum dots into the waterborne polyurethanes to construct photoluminescence films and demonstrated their potential applications prospect in the fields of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. This work broadens the avenue for fabricating room-temperature self-healing and multifunctional waterborne polyurethanes.

head.ce:title: Mechanically robust and fast room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane constructed by coordination bond and hydrogen bond with antibacterial and photoluminescence functions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is still a great challenge to synthesize waterborne polyurethane (WPU) with excellent mechanical performance and fast room-temperature self-healing ability because of the mutually exclusive nature of these properties. Herein, a room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane based on Zn2+-carboxyl coordination bond and hydrogen bond was prepared by adding zinc ion buffer solution to WPU emulsion. Due to the synergistic effect of dynamic coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds, the optimized sample (WPU-Zn-3) exhibited excellent mechanical properties (a tensile strength of 7.26 MPa, a elongation at break of 855 % and an exceptional toughness of 26.76 MJ/m3) and fast room-temperature self-healing capacity under ethanol induction (a high healing efficiency of 88 % within 4 h), showing a good balance between fast room temperature healing ability and excellent mechanical properties. Interestingly, this method also works for nickel ions but not for iron and aluminum ions. Furthermore, compared with zinc chloride and zinc nitrate, the waterborne polyurethane containing zinc acetate showed the best comprehensive performance. Due to the presence of zinc ions, the sterilization rates of WPU-Zn-3 film samples against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus were 97.1 % and 97.8 %, respectively. Specially, we introduced graphene quantum dots into the waterborne polyurethanes to construct photoluminescence films and demonstrated their potential applications prospect in the fields of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. This work broadens the avenue for fabricating room-temperature self-healing and multifunctional waterborne polyurethanes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of people's awareness of environmental protection, waterborne polyurethanes (WPUs) using water as the dispersion medium are considered as substitutes for traditional solvent-based polyurethanes [1–3]. WPUs exhibit attractive properties including resistance to low temperature, excellent flexibility, wear-resistant and corrosion-resistant, which make them widely used in protective coating [4,5], textiles [6], biomaterials [7], soft electronic devices [8,9], etc. However, WPUs inevitably suffer mechanical damages during use, and these damages can shorten their service life. Therefore, the introduction of self-healing function into WPUs is an effective way to improve their durability and reduce their maintenance costs [10–14].

Generally, the self-healing mechanisms of WPUs include extrinsic self-healing and intrinsic self-healing. Compared with extrinsic self-healing, intrinsic self-healing, mainly realized by the breaking and recombination of dynamic covalent bonds or noncovalent bonds, which has the advantages of simple preparation process, no need for repairing agents, and repeatable healing [15–17]. Since most of waterborne polyurethanes are used at room temperature, the intrinsically self-healable WPUs with fast repairing speed at room temperature are beneficial to practical applications especially for protective coating, which can put into effect to self-heal the damages of material in time without additional high temperature [18–20]. Compared with dynamic covalent bonds, the dynamic noncovalent bonds with the ability to be dissociated and associated rapidly at room temperature aroused from their lower bond energy, have received more and more attention and room-temperature self-healing WPUs based on dynamic noncovalent bonds have gained extensive research [21–23].

Coordination bonds are typical dynamic noncovalent bonds with strengths between van der Waals interactions and covalent bonds and, importantly, coordination bonds can be tuned in a rather broad range due to a variety of easily available ligands and metal ions (Mn+), which makes them very attractive in the fields of room-temperature self-healing materials [24–28]. Up to now, the room-temperature self-healing polymer materials based on coordination bonds have been developed rapidly. Guo et al. [27] synthesized a room-temperature self-healing biomass-derived elastomers based on coordination interactions between the histidine moieties and metal ions by adding metal salt ethanol solution to polyoleic acid containing histidine groups dichloromethane (DCM) solution. After healing at room temperature for 1 h, the tensile strength healing efficiency of the damaged biomass elastomer exceeded 100 %, but the original tensile strength and elongation at break of the material were only 3.9 MPa and 231 %. Lai et al. [29] designed a room-temperature self-healing PDMS polymer based on Zn-Hbimcp (Hbimcp = 2,6-bis((imino)methyl)-4-chlorophenol) coordination bonds by mixing zinc chloride ethanol solution and Hbimcp-PDMS DCM solution, the as-prepared Zn(Hbimcp)2-PDMS polymer exhibited high stretchability (up to 2400 % strain) and high toughness (29.3 MJ m−3). After damage, 98.9 % of the original fracture strain could be recovered within 24 h at room temperature. However, the healing time (24 h) was relatively long. Although coordination bonds have been successfully introduced into polymer materials to achieve self-healing at room temperature, there is still much room for improvement in their mechanical properties and self-healing rates, and their preparation process required large amounts of toxic or environmentally harmful organic solvents. Hence, it is still a significant challenge to develop mechanically robust, fast room temperature self-healing water-based polymer materials, especially environmentally friendly waterborne polyurethanes based on coordination bonds. To the best of our knowledge, room temperature self-healing WPU based on coordination bonds has not been reported yet.

As is well known, the carboxyl groups on the molecular chain play a decisive role in maintaining the stability of the waterborne polyurethane emulsions [30]. Therefore, it is difficult to directly mix metal ion solution and polyurethane emulsion to prepare self-healing polyurethanes based on coordination bonds, which will lead to the rapid aggregation and precipitation of emulsion particles due to the strong interaction between metal ions and carboxyl groups. In order to solve this problem, Sheng et al. [31] designed an “inner soft external hard” self-healing waterborne poly(urethane-urea) (WPUU) coating film containing dual metal coordination bonds through an ion permeation strategy. In short, WPUU coating film was first covered by ZnCl2-ethanol solution. With ethanol volatilization, zinc ions further penetrated from the high concentration region to the low concentration region, finally forming a gradient coordination bonds structure. However, this method was complicated and difficult to control along with using a large amount of solvent. Additionally, the uneven distribution of coordination bonds in WPUU film may hinder its application in other fields. Wang et al. [32] developed a facile strategy to prepare high performance recyclable WPU materials by incorporating ammonia solution of Zn(OAc)2 into WPU emulsion, in which ammonia water could effectively avoid the immediate interaction between zinc ions and carboxyl groups, thereby maintaining the stability of the WPU emulsion. Moreover, with the volatilization of ammonia water, zinc ions could gradually form uniformly distributed coordination bonds with carboxyl groups in the polyurethane matrix during the film formation process. However, the focus of the study was to demonstrate that carboxylic‑zinc ion interactions undergo dynamic exchange reactions at high temperatures (125 °C), allowing the recovery of cross-linked polymers by hot pressing. Unfortunately, the application and expansion of this method to achieve the room temperature self-healing of waterborne polyurethane were not dealt with yet. Particularly, the stability of the WPU emulsion, mechanical properties and self-healing capability at room temperature of the WPU films with different metal ion content and various metal salts still need to be further studied, which is of great significance to the application and development of room temperature self-healing polyurethane based on coordination bonds. In addition, WPUs with a single self-healing function cannot commendably meet people's production and living needs. Therefore, endowing WPUs with more functions such as antibacterial and photoluminescence is significant to expand their application range and increase their added value.

In view of the above analysis, a novel facile fabricated method was designed to prepare room temperature self-healing WPUs based on coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds without using a large amount of organic solvents. As shown in Scheme 1 , ammonia water was first dropped into the Zn2+ ions aqueous solution to prepare a zinc ammonia complex ([Zn(NH3)4]2+) buffer solution. After that, a series of room temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethanes (WPU-Zn-x) containing zinc ions were prepared by adding the buffer solutions with different contents of zinc ions into the WPU emulsions respectively. The strong coordination effect between zinc ions and ammonia weakened the interaction between zinc ions and carboxyl groups on the surface of latex particles, thereby maintaining the stability of the emulsion. In the process of film formation, with the volatilization of ammonia, zinc ions was gradually released from the complex, and interacted with the carboxyl groups of the molecular chain to form uniformly distributed coordination bonds. With the synergism of dynamic coordination bonds and intrinsic hydrogen bonds of waterborne polyurethanes, the optimized sample film possessed self-healing efficiency of 88 % within 4 h at room temperature with the aid of ethanol and excellent mechanical properties with a tensile strength of 7.26 MPa, a elongation at break of 855 % and a toughness of exceptional 26.76 MJ/m3, which exceed most room-temperature self-healing polymer materials base on coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds, as shown in Table S1. Furthermore, the other metal salts (e.g. AlCl2, FeCl3, Ni(OAc)2, ZnCl2, and Zn(NO3)2) were also introduced into the WPU system in the same way to verify the effects of this novel fabricated method on the emulsions stability, and mechanical and self-healing properties of films. Meanwhile, zinc ions delivering their decent antibacterial properties to the films were approved. Finally, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were incorporated into WPU-Zn-x matrix to prepare WPU-Zn-x /GQDS photoluminescence composite films. The WPU/GQDs films, favorably combining the merits of WPU and GQDs, were successfully made into a Fu character and a bar code with the stealth effect through a cutting and splicing strategy, demonstrating their potential information encryption and anti-counterfeiting properties.

A series of waterborne polyurethane emulsions with different hard segment contents were synthesized, and their formulations are shown in Table S2. Taking WPU-4 as a typical example, the synthetic route is shown in Fig. S1. The required amounts of IPDI (6.67 g, 30 mmol), PTMG (20 g, 10 mmol), DMPA (1.34 g, 10 mmol) and catalyst (DBTDL) (0.02 wt% based on the mass of total monomers) were firstly charged into a dried four-necked flask with a reflux-condenser, a mechanical stirrer, a thermometer and a nitrogen inlet. In the presence of N2 atmosphere, the mixtures reacted for 3 h at 80 °C under stirring (250 r/min) to obtain the NCO-terminated prepolymer. Afterwards, the reaction temperature was cooled to 60 °C, and then BDO (0.45 g, 5 mmol) dissolved in butanone was introduced into system to further react with the NCO-terminated prepolymer for another 4 h for chain extension. After the reaction temperature was cooled to 40 °C, TEA (0.87 g, 8.6 mmol) was added to neutralize the carboxyl groups with stirring for 30 min. Whereafter, 68.43 g of deionized water was poured into the flask for emulsification under vigorous stirring (1500 r/min) for 15 min, followed by stirring at 500 r/min for 1.5 h. Finally, a solid content of ∼30 % WPU emulsion was obtained after butanone in the WPU was removed by rotary evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of WPU-M emulsions and films
ce:section.ce:para: The WPU emulsions containing Zn2+ were prepared by blending the above resulted WPU-4 emulsions and the zinc ammonia complex ([Zn(NH3)4]2+) buffer solution. The detailed recipe is summarized in Table S3. The process was as follows: a certain amount of zinc acetate was firstly dissolved in deionized water, and then extra ammonia solution was added to obtain [Zn(NH3)4]2+ buffer solution. Subsequently, [Zn(NH3)4]2+ buffer solution and WPU emulsion were mixed under constant stirring (150 r/min) for 30 min. The finally obtained WPU emulsion containing Zn2+ was named as WPU-Zn-x, where x represents the molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl group in WPU emulsion. The molar ratios of Zn2+ to carboxyl group in WPU were set as 0, 1:10, 1:8, 1:6, 1:4, 1:2, respectively, and the corresponding film samples were encoded as WPU-Zn-0, WPU-Zn-1, WPU-Zn-2, WPU-Zn-3, WPU-Zn-4 and WPU-Zn-5. For ease of comparison, WPU-4 is renamed WPU-Zn-0 here. Among them, the WPU-Zn-0 was served as the control sample. Then the as-prepared WPU-Zn emulsions were cast into Teflon mold and cured for 1 week at room temperature, followed with continuous drying at 80 °C for 12 h to prepare WPU-Zn films. The thickness of obtained films was about 0.5 mm. Similarly, other metal salts including AlCl3, FeCl3, Ni(OAc)2, ZnCl2, and Zn(NO3)2 were also added to WPUs according to the above procedure to prepare composite emulsions and films and the ratio of different metal ions to carboxyl groups was 1:6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of WPU/GQDs (or GO) composite emulsions and films
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of GQDs (0.25 wt% based on the mass of solid content of emulsion) was added into the as-prepared WPU-Zn-3 emulsion and ultrasonically treated for 30 min to obtain homogeneous WPU/GQDs composite emulsion. After that, the prepared composite emulsion was poured into the polytetrafluoroethylene mold, dried at room temperature for 1 week, and further dried at 50 °C for 24 h. The preparation process of WPU/GO composite emulsion and film was the same as that of WPU/GQDs, except that graphene quantum dots were replaced by GO.

Mechanical test was performed on a AI-7000S (Gotech, Taiwan) universal testing machine according to the ASTM D-638 standard with a strain rate of 100 mm min−1 at room temperature and each sample was cut into a dumbbell-shaped specimen with a dimension of ∼50 mm × 4 mm × 0.5 mm. Measurements were performed on five times of each group sample, and the average value (tensile stress and strain at break) was calculated.

The self-healing capacity of samples was evaluated through the healing degree of mechanical properties and crack. The polarized optical microscope (Leica DMLP, Germany) was used to observe the variation of crack morphologies before and after healing. The tensile tests were used to quantitatively study healing efficiency. Healing efficiency (η) was defined as the ratio of tensile stress between the healed sample (σ) and original sample (σ0) and calculated by the following formula (1):
(1)
η =
σ
σ 0
× 100 %

The antibacterial properties of the film samples were determined by the previously reported shaking flask method [33]. Escherichia coli (E.coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus) were first grown aerobically in sterile Luria–Bertani (LB) broth at 37 °C for 24 h and further diluted to 105 CFU/ml. Then, 10 mL bacterial culture (105 CFU/ml) was added into sterile test tube containing 0.5 g film samples. The above mixture is cultured on a shaker (250 rpm) at 37 °C for 5 h. After that, 100 μL of culture was diluted gradiently, and then100 μL diluted bacterial suspension was inoculated onto an agar plate using the smearing method. After incubated for 12 h at 37 °C, the number of colonies was counted. The sterilization rate (%) of bacteria was evaluated by using Eq. (2):
(2)
Sterilization rate
%
=
A − B
A
× 100 %
where A and B are the survival number of the bacterial colonies observed after the treating of WPU-Zn-0 and WPU-Zn film samples, respectively. Three times tests were carried out for each group sample and the average value was taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of WPU-Zn emulsions and films
ce:section.ce:para: The main problem for the fabrication of WPU emulsions containing Zn2+ ions is the formation of coagulation and gel-like structure with the directly addition of Zn2+ion aqueous solution to WPU emulsion originating from rapid crosslinking effect of metal ions and carboxyl groups in polyurethane molecular chain, as shown in Fig.S2. To overcome this problem, ammonia solution was firstly incorporated into Zn2+queous solution to form zinc ammonia complex ([Zn(NH3)4]2+) buffer solution due to the intense coordination effect between zinc ion and ammonia hinders its coordination with carboxyl group. As shown in Fig. 1a, the microscopic morphology of the WPU-Zn-3 emulsion remains roughly spherical structure with good dispersion after introducing [Zn(NH3)4]2+ buffer solution with an average particle size of 73.53 nm. To systematically investigate the effect of the amount of Zn2+ on the properties of the emulsions, we prepared a series of WPU emulsions with different molar ratios of Zn2+ to carboxyl groups and the corresponding data are shown in the Table S4, and the appearance of WPU-Zn emulsion is shown in Fig. S3. With the increase of the molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl group from 1:10 to 1:4, the appearance of emulsion gradually changes from light blue to pale white and finally to milky white due to the increase of average particle size from 56.49 nm to 82.91 nm. In addition, all WPU-Zn emulsion presents a unimodal size distribution, indicating the outstanding dispersion capacity (Fig. 1b). However, when the molar ratio of zinc to carboxyl reaches 1/2, the gel appears in WPU-Zn emulsion (Table S4 and Fig. S3). This phenomenon illustrates that [Zn(NH3)4]2+ still slightly interacts with carboxyl group, but the effect is much weaker than zinc ions. As shown in Fig. 1c, both the average diameter and zeta potential value of WPU-Zn emulsion increase with the increasing of molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl group, which further demonstrates ionic interactions happened between carboxyl group of emulsion particles surface and [Zn(NH3)4]2+ at the nanoscale. According to the dlov theory, the stability of emulsion is closely related to the van der Waals attraction and electrostatic repulsion between particles. If the repulsion force is greater than the attraction force, the emulsion is stable, otherwise the emulsion is unstable. The incorporating of [Zn(NH3)4]2+ instead of Zn2+ could lead to the decrease of Debye length and electrostatic repulsions. Zeta potential is defined as the electrical potential at the slipping plane, which decreases with the decrease of Debye length [34]. Therefore, the WPU-Zn emulsion stability decreases with the increasing of molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl group, which is manifested as the increase of the emulsion particle size. Based on comprehensive analysis of the above results, it could be inferred that the introduction of ammonia and the appropriate amount of zinc ions are the prerequisite to ensure the good stability of the emulsion.

In the film-forming process, Zn2+ gradually dissociated from the [Zn(NH3)4]2+, and interacted with carboxyl groups on the polyurethane molecular chain to form dynamic reversible coordination bonds, while the urethane and urea groups on the polyurethane molecular chain form single and double hydrogen bonds, respectively (Scheme 1). To further investigate the interaction process between [Zn(NH3)4]2+and emulsion, we chose WPU-Zn-0 and WPU-Zn-2 for comparative study. The appearance of WPU-Zn-0 and WPU-Zn-2 emulsions before and after heating at 80 °C for 3 h was observed, and their corresponding particle sizes and zeta potentials were measured. A small hole with a diameter of 2 mm was made in the cap of the sample vial to discharge water vapor and ammonia gas. As can be seen from the Fig. S4, the appearance of WPU-Zn-2 emulsion after heating at 80 °C for 3 h became cloudy and a small amount of gel appeared. Compared with WPU-Zn-2, the appearance of the WPU-Zn-0 emulsion did not change significantly. In addition, Fig. S5a shows that the absolute values of the Zeta potential of the emulsions decrease after heating at 80 °C for 3 h, which may be due to the volatilization of ammonia water and part of the zinc ions were cross-linked with carboxyl groups. It is worth noting that compared with WPU-Zn-0, the emulsion particle size of WPU-Zn-2 is significantly increased due to the cross-linking of zinc ions and carboxyl groups, which is consistent with the change in the appearance of the emulsion (Fig. S5b). In addition, after film formation at 80 °C, the mechanical strength of WPU-Zn-4 is much higher than that of WPU-Zn-0, which is presented later in the article. Based on the comprehensive analysis, we infer that during the heating process at 80 °C, with the volatilization of ammonia water, zinc ions were gradually released from zinc ammonium ions and underwent cross-linking reaction with carboxyl groups on the surface of particles.

In order to confirm the formation of coordination bond in WPU-Zn films, the structures of the films were characterized by attenuated total reflectance fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR). Compared with the spectrum of WPU-Zn-0, a new absorption peak appears at 1617 cm−1 in WPU-Zn-4 which suggests the formation of Zn-carboxyl coordination bond, as shown in Fig. 2a [35]. It is well known that waterborne polyurethane materials widely exist urea and carbamate groups, which were easy to form hydrogen bonds. The absorption peaks at 3336 and 1703 cm−1 are ascribed to hydrogen-bonded NH bond and CO, respectively [36]. Information on other characteristic peaks can be found in supporting materials. These characteristic peaks indicate the successful synthesis of WPU-Zn films.

In addition, the transmittance of the film samples with a thickness of about 0.5 mm was characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy. It is clearly seen from Fig. 2b that the light transmittance of WPU-Zn films decreases gradually with the increase of the molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl groups. The reason is that increasing coordination interaction between zinc ions and carboxyl groups lead to an increment in aggregation of hard segments, which hinders the transmission of light. Atomic force microscope (AFM) analysis was applied to study degree of phase separation of WPU-Zn sample [37]. A tapping mode was used to present topographic features of sample surfaces by height imaging. The surface of WPU-Zn-3 appears to be irregular with many small bulges as shown in Fig. 2c. The existence of these small bulges can be explained by the notion that zinc ion coordinates with carboxyl groups to induce aggregation of WPU molecular chains, which results in microphase separation. This result is consistent with the previous film transmittance analysis. As shown in Fig. S6, with the increase of the molar ratio of zinc ion to carboxyl groups, the bulges become more obvious, indicating that the degree of microphase separation is improved, which is conducive to the improvement of the mechanical properties of the film.

To investigate the effect of zinc ion content on thermal behavior of WPU-Zn films, the glass transition temperature (Tg) was measured by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA). As depicted in Fig. 3a and Fig. 3c, the Tg values measured by DMA are higher than those measured by DSC due to the difference of test principle between the two methods [38]. Nevertheless, the change trends of Tg value are consistent in these two methods. With the increase of the ratio of zinc ions to carboxyl groups, the Tg value of WPU-Zn films increases, implying that the mobility of the polymer chain is decreased. This is mainly because the coordination bonds limited the movement of molecules chains. The obtained Tg values of all of the WPU-Zn films are well below the room temperature, suggesting that the polymer chains possess good movement ability at room temperature. Moreover, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves were performed to evaluate the thermal stability and decomposition behaviors of the WPU-Zn films. The represented thermal degradation data were summarized in Table S5. The temperature at 5 % (T 5%)weight loss is usually used to characterize the thermal stability of materials [39]. It can be seen Fig. 3b and Table S5 that T 5% moves towards higher temperatures with the increasing of Zn2+ content and compared to 277 °C for WPU-Zn-0, the T 5% of WPU-Zn-4 reaches to 287 °C. As shown in Fig. S7, the degradation process of all polyurethane films is mainly divided into two stages. The first stage mainly corresponds to the decomposition of hard segment, while the second stage corresponds to the decomposition of soft segment. In addition, the results in Table S5 showed that the temperature of the maximum degradation rate for both soft and hard segments has increased with the addition of Zn2+ ion. The improvement in thermal stability can be attributed to the breaking of strong coordination bonds between Zn2+ and carboxyl groups needs more heat and the uniformly dispersed zinc ions can delay the attack of the heat source on the hard and soft sections [40].

In order to examine the effect of Zn2+ on the mechanical properties of materials, the uniaxial tensile tests were carried out. The represented stress-strain curves of WPU-Zn films with different molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl group are shown in Fig. 4b and their mechanical properties including tensile stress, strain at break, Young's modulus and toughness were summarized in Table S6. With regard to WPU-Zn-0, the tensile stress and Young's modulus are only 1.71 MPa and 1.78 MPa respectively, due to its linear structure. However, its strain at break can reach ∼1320 %. When the molar ratio of Zn2+ to carboxyl group in WPU reaches 1:4, WPU-Zn-4 displays tentile stress of 11.08 MPa and Young's modulus of 5.96 MPa, which are more than six times and three times that of WPU-Zn-0, respectively. Nevertheless, compared with WPU-Zn-0, the strain at break of WPU-Zn-4 decreases sharply from 1320 % to 826 %. The significant improvement of mechanical properties is closely related to the formation of coordination bonds between Zn2+ and carboxyl groups. The coordination bonds acting as the stronger crosslinking site strengthen the interactions of molecular chains, thereby improving the tentile stress and Young's modulus of the materials, however limiting the sliding and unfolding of chain segments. Moreover, the fracture toughness of WPU-Zn-4 reaches a maximum value of 37.53 MJ/m3 which is more than three times than that of WPU-Zn-0 (10.53 MJ/m3), indicating the toughening effect of the Zn2+. The hysteresis behavior reflecting energy dissipation of the material during a loading and unloading cycle could be also used to evaluate the toughness of the materials [40,41]. Therefore, the stretch-recovery measurements of WPU-Zn samples were carried out at the strain of 300 %. As shown in Fig. 4c, the stretch-recovery profiles of all samples have obvious hysteresis loops, indicating that the energy is dissipated through dynamic bonds fracture in the process of cycling. In order to quantitatively study the change of dissipated energy, we obtained the toughness value by calculating the area of hysteresis loop, as depicted in Fig. 4d. The toughness of WPU-Zn-0 is only 0.85 MJ/m3 at the strain of 300 %, which mainly depends on the fracture of weak hydrogen bonds of the system to dissipate energy. With the increase of the molar ratio of zinc ion to carboxyl group, the toughness of the material improves gradually. When the molar ratio of Zn2+to carboxyl group reaches 1:4, the toughness value of WPU-Zn-4 reaches 3.87 MJ/m3 at the strain of 300 %, which is 4.5 times that of WPU-Zn-0. This result can be explained by Fig. 4a, the stronger coordination bond formed by zinc ion and carboxyl group could also be broken to dissipate the energy of the system during the cycle process resulting in improving of toughness. The WPU-Zn-3 served as typical sample was conducted on stretch-recovery test at the strain of 300 % for different waiting time. It can be seen from Fig. 4e that WPU-Zn-3 has obvious hysteresis loop, indicating that the fracture of hydrogen bond and coordination bond could effectively dissipate energy in the first loading/unloading cycle. As shown in Fig. 4f, the tensile stress and toughness (area of hysteresis loop) are 1.93 MPa and 2.73 MJ/m3, respectively. However, the area of hysteresis loop (0.50 MJ/m3) and tensile stress (1.31 MPa) of the second cycle without rest significantly lower than those of the first cycle. This is because the destroyed hydrogen and coordination bonds cannot be rebuilt immediately. After relaxing for 1 h, the stretch-recovery behavior of WPU-Zn-3 is mostly restored. The tensile stress and toughness of the third cycle are 1.68 MPa and 2.02 MJ/m3, which are 87.04 % and 73.99 % of the first cycle values, respectively. This observation shows that the dynamics properties of hydrogen bond and coordination bond enable them to be broken and recombined rapidly in a short time, so as to restore the mechanical properties of the materials [42]. Moreover, we observed the fatigue resistance of the samples through six consecutive loading and unloading experiments at the strain of 300 %. Fig. 4g displays that the tensile strength and hysteresis loop area of the second cycle shows a sharp decline, and this downward trend slows down in the subsequent cycle, which again illustrates that the dynamic bonds destroyed in the first cycle process cannot be reconstructed immediately. After relaxing for 24 h, the stretching and recovering paths are similar to the original samples and both the tensile strength and hysteresis area almost completely recover, demonstrating the excellent fatigue resistance of WPU-Zn-3. The above results demonstrate that the strong coordination bonds between Zn2 + and carboxyl groups and the weak hydrogen bonds of urethane or urea groups can form the hierarchical physical crosslinking between different molecular chains, so as to maintain the high strength and stretchable supramolecular polyurethane network.

In order to obtain WPU-Zn films with good self-healing properties at room temperature, we first optimized the WPU formulations. The mechanical properties and room-temperature self-healing efficiency of WPU samples were tested. It can be seen from Fig. S8a that as the hard segment contents decrease, the tensile strength of the WPU films gradually decrease while their strain increase. At the same time, the self-healing efficiency of the WPU films gradually improves, as shown in Fig. S8b. This is because the decline of the hard segment contents reduces the motion restriction effect on the flexible segment, which is beneficial to the diffusion and winding of the chain segment on the fracture surface. The room temperature self-healing efficiency of WPU-1, WPU-2 and WPU-3 is relatively low (<55 %). Although WPU-5 has the highest self-healing efficiency (85 %) at room temperature, its tensile strength is only 0.71 MPa. In view of this, we chose WPU-4 as the base emulsion and introduce metal ions for further study.

To study the self-healing capability of WPU-Zn film sample, we observed its self-healing behavior macroscopically. Taking WPU-Zn-3 as an example, the room-temperature self-healing process induced by trace ethanol is shown in the Fig. 5a. Two dumbbell samples of WPU-Zn-3 are cut into two completely separated parts using razor blade and immediately re-spliced (one of the samples was dyed pink for easy observation). After contacting for 4 h at room temperature with the assistance of 2 μL ethanol, the two pieces of WPU-Zn-3 achieve self-healing. A small amount of ethanol does not affect the smoothness of the incision. Moreover, the healed WPU-Zn-3 sample exhibits superior tensile property, which could even be stretched up to 600 % without breaking (Movie S1), demonstrating its excellent room-temperature self-healing ability. In addition, the fractured round WPU-Zn-3 sample with diameter of 50 mm and thickness of 1.5 mm can lift up 5 kg dumbbell after healing at room for 4 h, suggesting the healed sample still has good mechanical properties, as shown in Fig. 5b and Movie S2. The excellent self-healing ability of the WPU-Zn-3 sample is also confirmed by the micrograph of the crack on the surface. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the cracks on the sample surface disappear completely after healing at room temperature for 4 h with the aid of ethanol.

In order to quantitatively evaluate the healing efficiency of samples, the tensile experiments were conducted. The represented stress-strain curves of original and healed WPU-Zn samples at room temperature for 4 h with the assist of ethanol are shown in Fig. S9. The self-healing efficiency is determined by the ratio of the tensile strength of the sample before and after healing. As can be seen from Fig. 6a, the self-healing efficiency of WPU-Zn-0 is only 54.9 %. This is because WPU-Zn-0 system mainly relied on hydrogen bond to realize self-healing, however the effect of hydrogen bond itself is too weak to maintain the mechanical strength of the repaired material, and the healing rate is low. The healing efficiency increases from 54.9 % to 88.3 % with the molar ratio of zinc ion to carboxyl group increasing from 0 to1:6. The reason can be attributed to the addition of zinc ions can form more dynamic coordination bonds, hence there is more dynamic cross-linking sites at the fracture surface to participate in the self-healing process. When the molar ratio is further increased to 1:4, the self-healing efficiency declines to 81.5 %, indicating that the formation of excessive coordination bonds restricts the mobility of polymer chain resulting in reducing of self-healing efficiency. Considering the three dimensions of tensile stress, toughness and healing time (healing efficiency>85 %), the comprehensive performance of WPU-Zn-3 is superior to most of the currently reported room-temperature self-healing polymers based on hydrogen bonds and coordination bonds as shown in Fig. 6c.

Considering WPU-Zn-3 possessed the highest self-healing efficiency, we further studied the effect of healing time on its self-healing efficiency. As shown in Fig. 6b, the tensile stress and strain at break of the WPU-Zn-3 gradually recover with the extension of healing time. The recovery ratios of tensile stress WPU-Zn-3 sample are 28.7 % for 1 h, 49.6 % for 2 h, 74.8 % for 3 h and 88.3 % for 4 h, respectively (Fig. S10). The results show that the increase of recovery rate slows down with the extension of time, which is closely related to the volatilization of ethanol during healing process. We further extended the healing time to 24 h and observed the healing efficiency of the sample. As shown in the Fig. S11, the self-healing efficiency of WPU-Zn-0 is obviously increased, while that of the WPU-Zn-3 is not. This may be because the chain motion of the sample containing coordination bonds decreases sharply after ethanol volatilization, which makes the subsequent dynamic bond network difficult to rearrange.

In order to reveal the key role of ethanol in the self-healing of samples at room temperature, the mechanical properties of damaged WPU-Zn-3 sample without ethanol stimulation were tested after 4 h of at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S12, the tensile stress of healed WPU-Zn-3 is only 1.55 MPa and its self-healing efficiency is 21.3 %. Based on the above analysis, we propose a reasonable ethanol-induced self-healing mechanism at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 6d. There are many broken hydrogen bonds and coordination bonds at the fracture surface after physical behavior damage. The ethanol solvent penetrates into the polyurethane network and acts as a plasticizer to promote the rapid diffusion and full entanglement of chain segments at the crack. At the same time, the broken dynamic hydrogen bonds and coordination bonds are reconnected after fast volatilization of ethanol. These two factors promoted the rapid reconstruction of the network at the fracture surface, thereby restoring of mechanical properties quickly [43–45].

The above research results show that the zinc ion buffer solution can ensure the stability of the WPU emulsion, and at the same time improves the thermal, mechanical and self-healing properties of the WPU film. We further observed whether this approach is applicable to other metal ions. First, the direct addition of Ni2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ solutions to WPU emulsions, respectively, leads to rapid aggregation and precipitation of latex particles, as shown in Fig. S13. This phenomenon is similar to the previous results after mixing the Zn2+ ion solution and the WPU emulsion. Therefore, we tried to avoid this situation by adding excess ammonia water to the aqueous solution of these metal ions to form buffer solutions, the phenomenon is shown in Fig. 7a. During the slow addition of excess ammonia, the zinc ion and nickel ion solution both appeared hydroxide precipitation first, and then the precipitation disappeared. This is because the hydroxide precipitate can continue to react with ammonia water to form soluble metal ammonia complex ions. However, the hydroxide precipitate formed by the reaction of iron ions and aluminum ions with ammonia water does not dissolve when the ammonia water is excessive. This phenomenon can be explained by the hard/soft-acid/base (HSAB) principle. It is well known that HSAB can describe the affinity between ions and ligands based on their relative sizes and charge densities [46]. Smaller ions with higher charge densities, called hard acids, tend to bind preferentially with smaller sized ligands called hard bases. Conversely, soft acids (larger ions with lower charge density) will preferentially interact with soft bases (large ligands) [47]. According to hard/soft-acid/base principle, Fe3+ ions and Al3+ ions are hard acids and more likely to combine with hard bases to form stable compounds. As a Lewis base, ammonia contains NH3 and OH−. Since OH− is harder than NH3, OH- reacted preferentially with Fe3+ ions and Al3+ ions to form precipitates. It is reasonable to speculate that other hard acid metal ions are not suitable for this method.

Subsequently, we added the nickel ion buffer solution into the WPU emulsion and coated it on the PTFE plate to form a film. The film forming process is shown in Fig. S14. During the drying process, the color of the WPU film changes from light blue to light green, indicating that ammonia is released from complex ions, which is beneficial to the subsequent interaction of nickel ions and carboxyl groups. This phenomenon further supports the previous film formation mechanism. In addition, we studied the effects of zinc salts with different counterions and Ni(OAc)2 on the mechanical properties of waterborne polyurethanes, as shown in Fig. S15 and Fig. 7b. The tensile strength of WPU-Ni is higher than that of WPU-Zn, which is determined by their conformation of coordination bond. The zinc ion d orbital is completely filled, so the ligand cannot bind to the non-bonding electrons of the d orbital, while the d orbital of the nickel ion is not filled, so the ligand can bind to the non-bonding electrons of the d orbital. In this case, the coordination stability of nickel ion and carboxyl group is higher than that of zinc ion and carboxyl group [48]. Of course, the crosslinking effect of zinc ion and carboxyl group is more dynamic, therefore, the self-healing efficiency of WPU-Zn is higher than that of WPU-Ni (Fig. 7c).

In addition, different Zinc salts were also introduced into waterborne polyurethane. The counterions of zinc salt have obvious effect on the mechanical properties of WPU films. It can also be seen Fig. 7b, the tensile stress WPU/Zn(OAc)2 is significantly higher than that of WPU/ZnCl2 or WPU/Zn(NO3)2. One possible reason is that strong acid anions are not conducive to the interaction of metal ions with weak acid anions on the polymer chain. Among different zinc salts, the WPU/Zn(OAc)2 has the best comprehensive performance. It can also be seen from Fig. 7c that the healing efficiency of the water-based polyurethane cross-linked by the four metal salts can reach >85 % after healing for 4 h at room temperature with the assistance of a small amount of ethanol.

The appearance and properties of waterborne polyurethane films are easily affected by bacteria, so it is of great significance to endow waterborne polyurethane coating with antibacterial function. It is exciting that the prepared WPU-Zn films have excellent antibacterial properties due to the presence of zinc ions. The antibacterial properties of WPU-Zn film with different Zinc ion content were tested by shake flask method using typical E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria). As depicted in Fig. 8a, the Zn2+ ions can adsorb on the surface of bacteria by electrostatic action to destroy the function of the cell membrane resulting in the outflow of cytoplasm and disturb the function of various enzymes or cell metabolism which eventually causes bacterial death [49,50]. Compared with WPU-Zn-0, the number of survival bacteria treated with WPU-Zn-2 and WPU-Zn-3 decreases sharply, indicating the introducing of zinc ions significantly improved the antibacterial ability of the films (Fig. 8b). Moreover, with the molar ratio of zinc ions to carboxyl groups increases from 1:8 to 1:6, the sterilization rate of E.coli increases from 86.2 % to 97.1 % and the sterilization rate of S.aureus increases from to 87.4 % to 97.8 %, demonstrating outstanding antibacterial properties (Fig. 8c).

Endowing waterborne polyurethane with photoluminescence function can further expand its application range including light-emitting diode, information storage, optical anti-counterfeiting and so on [51]. As a derivative of graphene material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with size <100 nm have unique photoluminescence properties due to boundary effect and confinement effect [52]. Therefore, graphene quantum dots were introduced into the above room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane materials (WPU-Zn-3) to prepare photoluminescence materials. It can be seen from the Fig. S16 that graphene and quantum dot solution are similar in color under visible light, while GQDs solution emits green light under ultraviolet light, which indicates the successful synthesis of GQDs. As show in Fig. S17, compared with pure emulsion and film of WPU-Zn-3, the emulsion and film WPU-Zn-3/GQDs emits bright light, suggesting GQDs endow WPU-Zn-3/GQDs nanocomposites with photoluminescence function. In order to demonstrate the application prospect of WPU-Zn-3/GQDs in the field of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. We cut WPU-Zn-3/GQDs film and WPU-Zn-3/GO film into regular small blocks with a blade, and then spliced them according to the preset scheme to form a whole through the self-healing ability of the polymer itself (Fig. 9a). As shown in Fig. 9b, we cannot find any information under visible light. Once it is exposed to ultraviolet light, the traditional element Fu character can be observed. Based on this strategy, WPU-Zn-3/GQDs film and WPU-Zn-3/GO film are also cut into strip shape to fabricate a bar code, which is used to distinguish the authenticity of expensive chemicals, as shown in Fig. 9c. Obviously, combining the self-healing property of WPU-Zn-3 film and the photoluminescence properties of GQDs provide a promising path for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated a room-temperature self-healing waterborne polyurethane(WPU) film based on coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds by mixing zinc ammonia complex buffer solution into WPU emulsion. With adjusting the content of zinc ion, the optimized WPU-Zn-3 showed excellent mechanical properties (tensile stress of 7.26 MPa, fracture strain of 855 % and toughness of 26.76 MJ/m3). Due to the dynamic characteristics of coordination bonds and hydrogen bonds and the inductive effect of ethanol, the tentile stress of damaged WPU-Zn-3 sample could be restored to 88 % of the original value within 4 h at room temperature. Importantly, room temperature self-healing polyurethanes constructed in this way are not limited to zinc ions, and the properties mentioned above could also be achieved by nickel ions. However, this method was not suitable for aluminum and iron ions. The anion of zinc salt has an important influence on the properties of waterborne polyurethane. Among them, the WPU containing zinc acetate showed the best comprehensive performance. In addition, WPU-Zn-3 possessed superior antibacterial ability with E. coli sterilization rate of 97.1 % and S. aureus sterilization rate of 97.8 % due to the presence of zinc ions. We specially introduced graphene quantum dots into waterborne polyurethane to prepare photoluminescence materials, and demonstrated their applications in information encryption and optical anti-counterfeiting.
